text: minimally invasive Ivor Lewis esophagectomy for esophageal cancer after gastric bypass, performed by the Division of Minimally Invasive Surgery at the University of Florida. Our patient is a fifty-five year old female, five years status post laparoscopic gastric bypass surgery, who was referred to our institution after endoscopy revealed a seven centimeter esophageal adenocarcinoma located at thirty centimeters from the incisors. Her PET-CT showed no distant disease, and an endoscopic ultrasound showed a T-III-N-I lesion. The patient received neoadjuvant chemoradiation, and a good response was observed on her follow-up PET-CT with a decrease in maximum SUV.

The surgeon places abdominal ports and completes adhesiolysis, identifying the roux limb and gastric remnant. The liver is freed from the roux limb for retraction, exposing the diaphragmatic hiatus. The gastric pouch and roux limb are dissected away from the gastric remnant, avoiding the gastrocolic omentum to prevent injury to the right gastroepiploic artery. The gastric remnant, gastric pouch, and roux limb are clearly visible.

The surgeon applies anterior traction on the gastric pouch, dividing its attachments up to the left gastric artery. The right crus is exposed, and the phrenoesophageal ligament is divided. Circumferential mobilization of the esophagus is carried cephalad into the mediastinum, with incisions made in the right crus and central tendon to widen the hiatus. Remaining adhesions on the left side of the gastric pouch are divided, completing the mobilization of the esophagus, gastric pouch, and roux limb.

The surgeon divides the jejunum distal to the gastrojejunostomy using an endoscopic linear stapler. The mesenteric attachments to the jejunum are separated, elevating the specimen to expose the left gastric artery. A celiac nodal dissection is performed by skeletonizing the left gastric artery and reflecting nodal tissue towards the stomach. The left gastric artery is transected with a linear stapler.

A Penrose drain is placed around the esophagus and secured. The surgeon mobilizes the greater curvature of the remnant stomach to construct the gastric conduit, preserving the gastroepiploic pedicle. A pyloroplasty is performed, and the conduit is shaped by stapling along the lesser curvature with multiple firings of an endoscopic linear stapler. The distal end of the specimen is sutured to the end of the gastric conduit for delivery into the thoracic cavity.
The surgeon places a 16 French jejunostomy tube into the roux limb and secures it to the abdominal wall, ensuring proper positioning and fixation.

The patient is positioned in the left lateral decubitus position, and thoracic ports are placed. The surgeon divides the inferior pulmonary ligament and incises the pleura along the azygos vein. The Penrose drain is delivered into the chest to aid in mobilizing the thoracic esophagus. The esophagus is circumferentially mobilized up to the azygos vein, with lymphatic tissues mobilized en bloc. The azygos vein is dissected and divided using a linear stapler with a curved tip. The proximal esophagus is transected with a stapler, and the gastric conduit is delivered into the chest. The specimen, including the esophagus, gastric pouch, and a portion of the jejunum, is removed.

The surgeon uses the Orville device to deliver the anvil into the proximal esophagus. A gastrotomy is created in the gastric conduit to fit a 25mm EEA stapler, which is then used to perform a stapler anastomosis. The gastrotomy is closed with a stapler, completing the esophagogastric anastomosis.

An esophagram performed on post-operative day number four demonstrates a well-formed conduit and no evidence of a leak. Final pathology revealed no residual tumor and negative surgical margins with eighteen negative lymph nodes. The patient did well and was discharged on post-operative day number seven.
